questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with a 3-day history of bilateral lower abdominal pain, deep dyspareunia, and a yellowish vaginal discharge. On examination, temperature 38.2°C, cervical motion tenderness, and adnexal tenderness. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The triad of bilateral lower abdominal pain, cervical motion tenderness, and mucopurulent discharge is classic for PID. Ectopic pregnancy would typically have amenorrhoea and a positive pregnancy test. Appendicitis is usually unilateral and right-sided. Endometriosis is chronic and often presents with dysmenorrhoea. Ovarian torsion presents with acute severe unilateral pain and vomiting.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman complains of a dull lower abdominal ache for 2 weeks, intermenstrual spotting, and painful periods. She is afebrile. Bimanual examination reveals mild cervical motion tenderness and no adnexal masses. Urine pregnancy test negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: PID can present subacutely with minimal fever. Cervical motion tenderness is a key distinguishing sign from endometriosis (which typically has nodularity on uterosacral ligaments) and IBS (no pelvic tenderness). Ovarian cyst and UTI would not cause cervical motion tenderness.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with sudden onset severe lower abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. She has a temperature of 39.0°C, guarding and rebound tenderness in the lower abdomen. Speculum examination reveals purulent cervical discharge. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The presence of purulent cervical discharge and cervical motion tenderness points to PID as the source of peritonitis. Appendicitis is typically unilateral and does not have cervical discharge. Perforated peptic ulcer is epigastric. Ruptured ectopic pregnancy would have haemodynamic instability and positive pregnancy test. Ovarian torsion pain is usually unilateral.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with a 1-week history of lower abdominal pain and vague discomfort. She has no vaginal discharge or bleeding. On examination, there is mild suprapubic tenderness but no cervical motion tenderness due to atrophy. She has a history of multiple sexual partners in the past. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Although less common postmenopausally, PID can occur due to residual infection or new STIs. The history of multiple partners raises suspicion. Ovarian malignancy often presents with bloating and mass. Diverticulitis is left-sided and associated with bowel changes. Endometriosis usually resolves after menopause.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with a copper intrauterine device (IUD) inserted 2 years ago presents with 5 days of lower abdominal pain, dyspareunia, and a foul-smelling vaginal discharge. Temperature 38.5°C. On examination, cervical motion tenderness and adnexal tenderness. Urine pregnancy test negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: IUD use increases the risk of PID. The findings of discharge, fever, and cervical motion tenderness are classic. IUD expulsion would have a missing or shortened string. Ectopic pregnancy is unlikely with negative pregnancy test. Ovarian torsion typically presents with unilateral pain. Septic abortion requires pregnancy.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old sexually active woman presents with lower abdominal pain and vaginal discharge. On examination, there is cervical motion tenderness and bilateral adnexal tenderness. What is the most appropriate initial investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Endocervical swab for Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae NAAT
questions.explanation: NICE recommends testing for Chlamydia and Gonorrhoea in suspected PID; endocervical NAAT is the most sensitive for these organisms. High vaginal swab is less sensitive for gonorrhoea. Ultrasound and laparoscopy are not first-line for diagnosis; CRP is non-specific.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with a copper intrauterine device (IUD) presents with pelvic pain and dyspareunia. She has no fever. On speculum examination, there is mucopurulent discharge from the cervical os. Which investigation should be performed to maximize diagnostic yield for the causative organism?
options: Remove IUD and culture the device
questions.explanation: Endocervical swab NAAT is the most sensitive test for chlamydia and gonorrhoea, even with an IUD in situ. Culture of IUD is not recommended. Urine NAAT is less sensitive. Vaginal swab may miss chlamydia. Ultrasound is not for organism identification.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman with suspected PID has negative endocervical NAAT for Chlamydia and Gonorrhoea. She continues to have lower abdominal pain and tenderness. Transvaginal ultrasound shows thickened, fluid-filled fallopian tubes. What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis of PID?
options: High vaginal swab for anaerobic culture
questions.explanation: Laparoscopy is the gold standard for diagnosis of PID when imaging and microbiology are equivocal. It allows direct visualization of tubal inflammation. Repeat NAAT unlikely to be positive; MRI not indicated; CA-125 non-specific; anaerobic culture from HVS is not reliable.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with known PID (diagnosed clinically) develops worsening pain and swinging pyrexia. She has a palpable adnexal mass on bimanual examination. Which imaging is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of tubo-ovarian abscess?
options: CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast
questions.explanation: Transvaginal ultrasound is first-line imaging for suspected tubo-ovarian abscess per NICE. CT may be used if ultrasound inconclusive or other acute pathology suspected. Plain X-ray not useful. MRI not first-line. Laparoscopy invasive and not first-line for diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman presents with lower abdominal pain and cervical motion tenderness. Endocervical NAAT for Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae is negative. What is the most appropriate next investigation to identify the causative organism?
options: High vaginal swab for anaerobic culture
Test for Mycoplasma genitalium from endocervical swab
questions.explanation: NICE recommends considering testing for Mycoplasma genitalium if chlamydia and gonorrhoea tests are negative. Anaerobic culture is not routinely performed. Ultrasound and laparoscopy do not identify organisms. Serology is not diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with a two-day history of lower abdominal pain and vaginal discharge. She is afebrile, with mild bilateral adnexal tenderness on bimanual examination. Her chlamydia and gonorrhoea swabs are positive. What is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic regimen for her pelvic inflammatory disease?
options: Intramuscular ceftriaxone 500 mg single dose plus oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 14 days
Oral azithromycin 1 g single dose alone
Intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g once daily plus oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for 14 days
Intravenous doxycycline 100 mg twice daily and metronidazole 500 mg three times daily
Oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily and metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for 14 days
questions.explanation: For mild-to-moderate PID, NICE recommends oral doxycycline plus metronidazole as first-line. Intramuscular ceftriaxone is added if gonorrhoea is suspected, but for chlamydia-positive PID, the oral dual regimen is sufficient. Intravenous therapy is reserved for severe disease.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman is admitted with severe lower abdominal pain, high fever (39.5°C), rebound tenderness, and cervical motion tenderness. CRP is 120 mg/L. No abscess is seen on ultrasound. What is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic regimen?
options: Intravenous cefoxitin 2 g every 6 hours plus probenecid 1 g orally
Intravenous gentamicin 7 mg/kg once daily plus clindamycin 900 mg three times daily
Intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g once daily, intravenous doxycycline 100 mg twice daily, and intravenous metronidazole 500 mg three times daily
Oral ofloxacin 400 mg twice daily plus metronidazole 400 mg twice daily
questions.explanation: Severe PID requires intravenous triple therapy: ceftriaxone, doxycycline, and metronidazole. Cefoxitin with probenecid is an alternative but not first-line. Gentamicin/clindamycin and ertapenem are used for complicated infections but are not preferred for PID. Oral therapy is inadequate for severe disease.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman who uses a copper intrauterine device (IUD) is diagnosed with mild pelvic inflammatory disease. She has not yet started antibiotics. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Start oral antibiotics alone and keep the IUD in situ
Remove the IUD and wait for culture results before starting antibiotics
Start intravenous antibiotics and schedule IUD removal in 48 hours
Remove the IUD immediately, then start oral antibiotics
Start oral doxycycline and metronidazole, and remove the IUD after antibiotics are initiated
questions.explanation: In PID with an IUD, the device should be removed after starting antibiotics to avoid worsening infection. Immediate removal without antibiotics risks dissemination. Starting antibiotics alone is insufficient as the IUD acts as a foreign body. Intravenous therapy is not required for mild disease, and waiting for cultures delays treatment.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old primigravida at 10 weeks gestation presents with lower abdominal pain, fever, and purulent cervical discharge. Bimanual examination reveals cervical motion tenderness. Which of the following antibiotics is contraindicated in her treatment?
questions.explanation: Doxycycline is a tetracycline and is contraindicated in pregnancy due to teratogenic effects (e.g., fetal bone and tooth discolouration). Ceftriaxone, metronidazole (though used cautiously), erythromycin, and azithromycin are considered safer alternatives.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with severe pelvic inflammatory disease has a known severe penicillin allergy. She requires intravenous therapy. What is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen?
options: Intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g once daily plus doxycycline and metronidazole with premedication
Intravenous ofloxacin 400 mg twice daily plus intravenous metronidazole 500 mg three times daily
Intravenous gentamicin 7 mg/kg once daily plus clindamycin 900 mg three times daily
Intravenous doxycycline 100 mg twice daily plus metronidazole 500 mg three times daily alone
questions.explanation: For severe PID in penicillin allergy, NICE recommends intravenous ofloxacin plus metronidazole. Ceftriaxone is contraindicated due to cross-reactivity. Doxycycline plus metronidazole alone is inadequate for severe disease. Gentamicin/clindamycin is an alternative but not first-line, and moxifloxacin is not first-line for severe PID.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents to the ED with a two-day history of lower abdominal pain and yellowish vaginal discharge. She is afebrile, and urine pregnancy test is negative. On examination there is mild lower abdominal tenderness but no rebound. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer her to the genitourinary medicine clinic
Take high vaginal and endocervical swabs for STI testing
Start oral doxycycline 100mg BD and metronidazole 400mg BD
questions.explanation: In suspected mild PID, empirical antibiotics should be started immediately after taking swabs; delaying treatment while awaiting results increases risk of complications. The nearest distractor is taking swabs, but both swabs and antibiotics can be done simultaneously; the key is to not delay antibiotic therapy.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman visits her GP with lower abdominal pain, excessive discharge, and fever of 38.5°C for three days. Examination reveals cervical motion tenderness and bilateral adnexal tenderness. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Take endocervical swabs and start oral antibiotics
Refer urgently to the local gynaecology team for admission
Prescribe oral metronidazole alone pending swab results
Arrange a pelvic ultrasound within 24 hours
questions.explanation: Fever >38°C indicates moderate-to-severe PID; according to NICE, these patients should be admitted for intravenous antibiotics and monitoring. The nearest distractor is prescribing oral antibiotics, which would be insufficient for this severity.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with a recent diagnosis of PID (treated with oral doxycycline and metronidazole) returns after one week complaining of sharp right upper quadrant pain. Her pelvic ultrasound is normal. She is afebrile and has mild right upper quadrant tenderness. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer to hepatology for further evaluation
questions.explanation: Right upper quadrant pain in a patient with PID raises suspicion of Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome (perihepatitis). CT abdomen is the first-line imaging to confirm perihepatic inflammation and exclude alternative diagnoses. The nearest distractor, laparoscopy, is more invasive and reserved for unclear cases.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the ED with high fever (39.5°C), hypotension (90/60 mmHg), tachycardia (110 bpm), and a rigid, tender lower abdomen. She has a two-week history of lower abdominal pain and foul discharge. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Administer a 500 mL bolus of normal saline
Transfer to the intensive care unit
Take blood cultures and start intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics
Obtain an urgent CT abdomen with contrast
questions.explanation: This patient has sepsis from severe PID. The Sepsis 6 guidelines mandate taking blood cultures and giving intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics within one hour. Fluids are also crucial but antibiotics are the definitive treatment for the infection. The nearest distractor, fluid bolus, is important but secondary to starting antibiotics.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with a copper intrauterine device (IUD) inserted 18 months ago presents with mild lower abdominal pain and increased discharge for four days. She is afebrile and has no cervical excitation. Urine pregnancy test is negative. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer her for urgent IUD removal and treat with oral antibiotics
Perform a pelvic ultrasound to assess for IUD malposition
Remove the IUD after starting antibiotics if symptoms are severe
Remove the IUD immediately and then start antibiotics
Start oral doxycycline 100mg BD and metronidazole 400mg BD without removing the IUD
questions.explanation: For mild PID in a patient with an IUD, current guidelines (e.g., RCOG) recommend treating with antibiotics alone; IUD removal is only indicated if severe infection or no improvement after 48 hours. The nearest distractor suggests removal after antibiotics, which would be appropriate for severe but not mild disease.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman presents with lower abdominal pain and vaginal discharge for 2 days. She has cervical motion tenderness and bilateral adnexal tenderness. Which single finding would be most concerning for a tubo-ovarian abscess?
questions.explanation: A palpable adnexal mass raises suspicion of a tubo-ovarian abscess, a serious complication of PID that may require drainage. Fever and tenderness are common in uncomplicated PID and do not specifically indicate an abscess.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute severe lower abdominal pain, vomiting, and light-headedness. She has not had a period for 6 weeks. On examination she is tachycardic and hypotensive. Which single finding must be urgently excluded?
questions.explanation: A positive pregnancy test in this setting is a red flag for ectopic pregnancy, which mimics PID and is life-threatening. Cervical motion tenderness and other signs are common to both conditions but do not exclude ectopic.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with pelvic inflammatory disease on oral antibiotics develops spiking fevers and right upper quadrant pain. Which clinical sign is most supportive of a diagnosis of Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome?
options: Rebound tenderness in the left lower quadrant
questions.explanation: Positive Murphy's sign (inspiratory arrest on palpation of the right upper quadrant) suggests perihepatitis (Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome) due to extension of pelvic infection. Jaundice and shoulder tip pain are less specific.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with a 2-week history of lower abdominal discomfort and watery vaginal discharge. Examination reveals cervical motion tenderness and mild adnexal tenderness. Which additional finding should prompt an urgent gynaecological ultrasound to exclude malignancy?
questions.explanation: Ascites in a postmenopausal woman with pelvic symptoms is a red flag for ovarian malignancy, which can mimic PID. An adnexal mass is also concerning, but ascites is more specific for advanced disease.

questions.stem: A 23-year-old woman with severe PID deteriorates with high fever, rigors, and confusion. Her blood pressure is 95/60 mmHg, heart rate 120 bpm, and respiratory rate 22/min. Which single laboratory finding would confirm red flag sepsis and indicate urgent escalation?
questions.explanation: A lactate >2 mmol/L is a red flag for sepsis, indicating tissue hypoperfusion and requiring immediate escalation. CRP and white cell count are non-specific markers of inflammation and do not define red flag sepsis.
